**Subjective**
**Chief Complaint**
Patient came in primarily for knee pain, needing an MRI referral.

**History of Present Illness**
Patient disclosed increased drinking over the past few months, transitioning from a weekend-only drinker (usually a glass of wine on a Friday night) to essentially nightly drinking (three to four standard drinks on drinking nights). She skips one to two nights per week but experiences a shift in pattern due to stressors.

The patient completed an AUDIT-C screening, revealing a pattern of drinking four to five times per week, two to three drinks on a typical day, sometimes four, and six or more drinks on one occasion once a month, resulting in an average of approximately fifteen to twenty drinks per week. This pattern is above the low-risk guidelines for women (no more than seven drinks per week and no more than three on any single occasion).

Patient identified significant psychosocial stressors, including her mother's stroke and coordination of care while working full time and her husband traveling frequently for work. She described the nightly wine as a way to take the edge off at the end of the day, reflecting a classic stress-driven pattern.

Patient attempted to stop drinking entirely last week but was unable to sustain it and was scared by the experience.

Patient was open and responded well to counseling options, including structured reduction strategies (capping at one drink on no more than three nights per week with alcohol-free days in between) and a thirty-day abstinence reset as an alternative approach. She was interested in addressing underlying stressors through counseling or therapy, specifically mentioning the BASICS program (brief alcohol intervention, two sessions, no labels).

On the medical side, a comprehensive metabolic panel, a CBC, and B12 and folate levels were ordered due to concerns about liver function and nutrient depletion from alcohol consumption.

The patient was reassured that it is likely fine but having a baseline is good. She was advised to keep a drinking diary over the next couple of weeks to help identify patterns and was referred for a BASICS program.

The plan is to follow up in approximately six weeks to review labs, check on the drinking diary, and see how she is doing with the BASICS referral and any counseling.

The patient was receptive throughout the conversation and is at a good stage of awareness for making some changes.
